informal: Prove that if $G$ is an abelian simple group then $G \cong Z_{p}$ for some prime $p$ (do not assume $G$ is a finite group).
lean:
theorem Dummit_Foote_exercise_3_4_1 (G : Type*) [CommGroup G] [IsSimpleGroup G] :
    IsCyclic G ∧ ∃ G_fin : Fintype G, Nat.Prime (@card G G_fin)
reasoning: The informal claim says any abelian simple group G is isomorphic to Z_p for some prime p. Being isomorphic to Z_p is equivalent to being cyclic and having prime (finite) order. The Lean statement assumes [CommGroup G] [IsSimpleGroup G] and concludes IsCyclic G together with the existence of a Fintype instance such that card G is prime. A cyclic group of prime cardinality is exactly Z_p, so the conjunction captures "G ≅ Z_p for some prime p" (the existential ∃ G_fin : Fintype G also encodes finiteness, matching the conclusion that G must be finite). No extra hypotheses (like assuming G finite up front) are imposed, and no part of the conclusion is weakened. Hence the formalization is logically equivalent to the informal statement.
